title: GDPR Records of Processing Activities Completeness

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.30 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.30 (Records of processing) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.8: GDPR Records of Processing Activities Completeness.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.8' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: CIM Network_Traffic and Authentication data models, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` (processing activities register), ES Asset Framework
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` dc(Authentication.user) as users, count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.dest
| rename Authentication.dest as dest
| lookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv system_name AS dest OUTPUT processing_activity, data_category, legal_basis, retention_period, last_review_date
| eval in_register=if(isnotnull(processing_activity), "Yes", "NOT_IN_ROPA")
| eval review_overdue=if(isnotnull(last_review_date) AND (now()-strptime(last_review_date,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400 > 365, "OVERDUE", "OK")
| where in_register="NOT_IN_ROPA" OR review_overdue="OVERDUE"
| sort - users
| table dest, users, count, in_register, processing_activity, legal_basis, review_overdue
description: Article 30 requires controllers to maintain documented records of processing activities including purposes, data categories, recipients, transfers, retention periods, and technical measures. This use case validates that the processing register (ROPA) is complete and current by cross-referencing it against observed data flows and systems, surfacing systems that process personal data but are not in the register — a gap regulators frequently cite during inspections.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` from your DPO's Article 30 register (exported from OneTrust, DataGrail, or manual spreadsheet); (2) compare active systems receiving authentication events against registered systems; (3) alert on systems with user activity not in the ROPA; (4) flag entries with reviews older than 12 months; (5) schedule quarterly for ROPA completeness auditing.
visualization: Table (unregistered systems), Single value (ROPA coverage %), Bar chart (users by unregistered system), Timeline (review dates).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: PII detections from authorized data exports, finance reports, support cases with logged PII, or anonymized data testing.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: CIM Network_Traffic and Authentication data models, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` (processing activities register), ES Asset Framework.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` from your DPO's Article 30 register (exported from OneTrust, DataGrail, or manual spreadsheet); (2) compare active systems receiving authentication events against registered systems; (3) alert on systems with user activity not in the ROPA; (4) flag entries with reviews older than 12 months; (5) schedule quarterly for ROPA completeness auditing.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` dc(Authentication.user) as users, count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.dest
| rename Authentication.dest as dest
| lookup gdpr_ropa_register.csv system_name AS dest OUTPUT processing_activity, data_category, legal_basis, retention_period, last_review_date
| eval in_register=if(isnotnull(processing_activity), "Yes", "NOT_IN_ROPA")
| eval review_overdue=if(isnotnull(last_review_date) AND (now()-strptime(last_review_date,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400 > 365, "OVERDUE", "OK")
| where in_register="NOT_IN_ROPA" OR review_overdue="OVERDUE"
| sort - users
| table dest, users, count, in_register, processing_activity, legal_basis, review_overdue
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Records of Processing Activities Completeness (Art. 30)** — Article 30 requires controllers to maintain documented records of processing activities including purposes, data categories, recipients, transfers, retention periods, and technical measures. This use case validates that the processing register (ROPA) is complete and current by cross-referencing it against observed data flows and systems, surfacing systems that process personal data but are not in the register — a gap regulators frequently cite during inspections.

Documented **Data sources**: CIM Network_Traffic and Authentication data models, `gdpr_ropa_register.csv` (processing activities register), ES Asset Framework. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **in_register** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **review_overdue** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where in_register="NOT_IN_ROPA" OR review_overdue="OVERDUE"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

See also the **CIM SPL** field in this use case; enable the matching data model acceleration if you run the tstats variant in production.

Output fields: count, dest, in_register, legal_basis, processing_activity, review_overdue, users.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (unregistered systems), Single value (ROPA coverage %), Bar chart (users by unregistered system), Timeline (review dates).
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of records of processing activities completeness — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.
